coredata.dc:title: Surface modification of SnO2 electron transporting layer by graphene quantum dots for performance and stability improvement of perovskite solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Panyathip, Rangsan
Sucharitakul, Sukrit
Hongsith, Kritsada
Bumrungsan, Wakul
Yarangsi, Vasan
Phaduangdhitidhada, Surachet
Chanlek, Narong
Choopun, Supab
coredata.dc:description: The surface modification of SnO2 electron transporting layer (ETL) is investigated for the performance and stability improvement of perovskite solar cells by adding citric acid (SnO2-citric) and graphene quantum dots (SnO2-GQDs) in SnO2 ETL precursors for carboxyl group and GQDs, respectively. As a result, the hydrophobic surface of SnO2-GQDs is obtained by adding GQDs to the SnO2 precursor, forming SnO2-GQDs ETL. In addition, the hydrophobic surface of SnO2 ETL is significant in increasing grain size crystallinity of perovskite obtained by this modification process. Also, the charge trap density and recombination carriers between ETL and perovskite interface were advanced. Thus, the surface modification of SnO2-GQDs ETL can enhance the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of PSCs at the highest value of 17.81% from 15.52% with a decrease of the hysteresis effect. The enhancement is due to the combining effect of carboxyl groups and GQDs interface engineering between ETL and perovskites layers. Also, the stability of perovskite solar cells without encapsulation for 28 days is achieved. These suggest that the combining effect can be used in surface modification and for efficiency enhancement of PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cells
Quantum dots
SnO2
Graphene
Electron transporting layer
Surface modification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface modifications of SnO2 ETL preparation
Surface modifications of SnO2 ETL characterization

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Size distribution of GQDs inset TEM result, XPS high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) FTIR spectra of GQDs in solution.

(a) High-resolution XPS spectra for Sn 3 d signals, (b) O1s signals, (c) UV–vis transmission spectra with an inset of the absorption spectra, and (d) XRD spectra of modified SnO2 film with citric acid and GQDs on FTO substrate.
Contact angle results of (a) unmodified SnO2, (b) SnO2-citric, and (c) SnO2-GQDs ETLs. Schematic of various wetting angles on ETL mechanism for (d) SnO2, (e) SnO2-citric, and (f) SnO2-GQDs ETLs.
SEM surface morphology films of (a) SnO2, (b) SnO2 -citric, and (c) SnO2-GQDs, the size distribution of perovskite grains on (d) SnO2, (e) SnO2-citric, and (f) SnO2-GQDs ETLs.
J-V in reverse scan on PSCs with different ETL conditions.

(a) Steady state of PL spectra for perovskite layer based on different ETLs, (b) dark I–V curves of the electron-only device displaying different V
TFL point behavior, (c) EIS results of PSCs based on different SnO2 ETLs at 100 kHz (inset; equivalent circuit) and (d) Bode phase plot of different ETLs.
Stability test of unencapsulated PSCs in different modified SnO2 ETLs devices (25 °C, 30% humidity).
Summarized PSCs parameters with different surface modified SnO2 ETL conditions.

head.ce:title: Surface modification of SnO2 electron transporting layer by graphene quantum dots for performance and stability improvement of perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Materials Science, Faculty of Science, Chiang Mai University, Chiang Mai, 50200, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Materials Science, Faculty of Science, Chiang Mai University, Chiang Mai 50200, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Synchrotron Light Research Institute (SLRI), 111 University Avenue, Nakhon Ratchasima, 30000, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Synchrotron Light Research Institute (SLRI), 111 University Avenue, Nakhon Ratchasima 30000, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The surface modification of SnO2 electron transporting layer (ETL) is investigated for the performance and stability improvement of perovskite solar cells by adding citric acid (SnO2-citric) and graphene quantum dots (SnO2-GQDs) in SnO2 ETL precursors for carboxyl group and GQDs, respectively. As a result, the hydrophobic surface of SnO2-GQDs is obtained by adding GQDs to the SnO2 precursor, forming SnO2-GQDs ETL. In addition, the hydrophobic surface of SnO2 ETL is significant in increasing grain size crystallinity of perovskite obtained by this modification process. Also, the charge trap density and recombination carriers between ETL and perovskite interface were advanced. Thus, the surface modification of SnO2-GQDs ETL can enhance the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of PSCs at the highest value of 17.81% from 15.52% with a decrease of the hysteresis effect. The enhancement is due to the combining effect of carboxyl groups and GQDs interface engineering between ETL and perovskites layers. Also, the stability of perovskite solar cells without encapsulation for 28 days is achieved. These suggest that the combining effect can be used in surface modification and for efficiency enhancement of PSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have gained extensive attention, and the state-of-the-art PSC currently exhibits a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of up to 26.1% [1] from 3.8% in 2009 [2]. The fabrication of PSCs has attracted a wide range of interest in the research and development of PSCs as commercial devices [3–8]. Typically, the PSCs fabrication is the multi-layer coating devices consisting of ETL, hole transporting layer (HTL), perovskite active layer, and metal electrode. In particular, the ETL is crucial in isolating photocurrent electrons and blocking hole carriers to improve electron carrier transfer [9]. SnO2 semiconductor material is commonly employed as ETL due to extraordinary electron mobility (240 cm2 V−1s−1) and compatible band energy alignment for highly efficient electron extraction [10,11]. Nonetheless, the SnO2 film for PSCs is often low-quality in the wet spin-coating process, which shows many sensitive defects from the environmental conditions leading to poor reproducibility. As a result, SnO2 film exhibits high-charge recombination, serving low performance and stability in PSCs, and is also difficult to produce as the commercialization of PSCs [12]. However, several techniques improve the quality of SnO2 film, including noble metals doping, multilayer composite ETL, and interface/surface modification [13]. The noble metals doping technique requires expensive noble metals ions dopant in SnO2 ETL [14,15], such as zirconium [16], ruthenium, niobium [14], and antimony [17], which increase the cost of the PSCs fabrication for commercialization. The multilayers of metal oxide are used and demonstrated to improve the quality of ETL, such as ALD-TiO2/SnO2/PCBM [18], HI-modified TiO2/SnO2 [19], ALD-TiO2/SnO2 [20], and In2O3/SnO2 [21]. Although the multilayer techniques promote the high efficiency of PSCs, the fabrication process could be more complex, more expensive, and more challenging to control during the scalable manufacturing process of PSCs.

Interface and surface modifications are a simple and low-cost technique for improving SnO2 ETL quality by surface morphology adjustment via treating and doping with molecules at the interfacial layers of ETL to modify the wettability of perovskite and defect passivation [22,23]. Recently, the surface modification of SnO2 ETL using chloroform-D treated on SnO2 nanoparticles has been reported [24]. The chlorine capped as SnO2 nanocrystals (SnO2–Cl) ETL is obtained and introduced to the non-wetting surface on ETL and increases grain sizes of perovskite resulting in the enhancement of the PSCs performance also decrease of charge trap density. Also, doping by adding a solution to the SnO2 precursor is frequently used for interface and surface modification. Adding KCl solution into SnO2 solution is reported [25], and it found that the K and Cl ions are passivated at ETL and perovskite interface via the defect passivation, leading to the decrease of charge carrier recombination at ETL and perovskite interface.

Additionally, surface modification by adding carboxyl group (−COOH) and fullerenes (C9, C60) to modify the SnO2 ETL [26,27] are investigated and reported. The carboxyl molecules from citric acid are applied in the SnO2 ETL, indicating the modification of morphology and decreased interfacial energy offset of SnO2 films [28]. Besides, the modified SnO2 films enhanced surface wettability to enable a better perovskite crystallization process, giving enlarged grain sizes of perovskite films and a smooth surface. These suggest the effect of carboxyl groups (−COOH) on the surface modification of SnO2 ETL, used to improve electronic properties and defect passivation.

GQDs are the carbon material in the 0-dimensional class that exhibits extremely high chemical stability, is environmentally friendly, biocompatible, and has large specific surface areas [29–31]. These are good candidates for the surface modification process at ETL film in PSCs [32]. GQDs exhibit the quantum size effect via exciton Bohr radius properties to encourage energy level band alignment of GQDs. Thus, surface modification by adding GQDs is used and investigated [33]. However, there is a rare report on the effect of both carboxyl groups and GQD on the surface modification of SnO2 ETL.

In this work, the combining effect of carboxyl group (-C-OH) and GQDs (C–COOH) molecules on the surface modification of SnO2 ETL by adding citric acid (SnO2-citric) and graphene quantum dots (SnO2-GQDs) into SnO2 ETL precursors for carboxyl group and GQDs, respectively, is intensively investigated. Then, the stability and performance improvement of perovskite solar cells are observed and discussed.

GQDs were synthesized via an electrochemical method using carbon source from graphite rods (5 mm diameter and 5 cm length (99.99% purity) as electrodes [34,35], which were cleaned in acetone with ultrasonication (5 min) and annealed at 450 °C for 15 min. An electrolytic solution (50 ml) was obtained by mixing 0.1 M (99.5%) citric acid into deionizing water (DI water). The electrochemical process is carried out by a 5 h direct voltage supply of 4–10 V (Agilent E3633A). Then, the dispersed GQDs were filtered with a 0.45 μm size filter before being added to the SnO2 precursor.

GQDs sizes were considered by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2010), observed chemical bonding of GQDs via x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI5000, Versa Probe II, ULVAC-PHI, Japan) used a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source to evaluate the chemical states and composition. Identification of functional group molecules in GQDs is examined by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700). In addition, GQDs are inspected by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis, Varian Cary 50) for light absorption properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modifications of SnO2 ETL preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) substrate (Great cell Solar Materials, 7 Ω sq−1 of sheet resistance) was etched for patterning by using a chemical reaction between Zn powder and HCl in deionized (DI) water for 1:1. The Alconox solution, acetone, ethanol, and DI water were used to clean the patterned FTO substrates in an ultrasonic bath for 15 min. The SnO2 ETL layer was fabricated by spin-coating SnO2 on the FTO conductive substrate. The SnO2 precursor (SnCl2·2H2O, 99.8%, Sigma Aldrich, Germany) was prepared by mixing SnCl2⋅2H2O (0.1 M) in ethanol. Then, SnO2 precursors (1.5 ml) were doped with a citric acid solution (0.1 M) and GQDs for 200 μL (1.5 ml: 200 μL), respectively. Next, each SnO2 precursor condition was coated into FTO substrates by spin-coating at 3000 rpm/30 s and heated at 180 °C/1 h in ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modifications of SnO2 ETL characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis technique measures the optical properties of SnO2 ETL. The surface morphology of SnO2 and perovskite film are observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6335 F). Also, the phase structure of SnO2 ETL films is analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku) with monochromatic Cu Kα radiation (2θ ranging from 30° to 70°). The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) of the perovskite layer on SnO2 ETL is examined by 470 nm excitation.

For PSCs preparation are used all chemical reagents are commercially available, N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF, anhydrous, 99.8%, Sigma Aldrich, Germany), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, ACS reagent, 99.9%, Sigma Aldrich), Chlorobenzene (CB, anhydrous 99.8%, Sigma Aldrich, Germany), 2-propanol (IPA, anhydrous 99.5%, Sigma Aldrich, Germany), and 4-tert-Butylpyridine (96%, Sigma Aldrich, Germany).

Lead iodide (PbI2, 99.999%, perovskite grade, Sigma Aldrich, Germany) and lead bromide (PbBr2, 99.999%, Sigma Aldrich, Germany), Formamidinium iodide (FAI, 99.99%, Great cell Solar Materials, Australia), and methylammonium bromide (MABr, 99.99%, Great cell Solar Materials, Australia). Lithium-bis(flurosulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI, 99.99%, Osiila, United Kingdom). Methylammonium chloride (MACl, 99.5%, Borun New Material Technology LTD, China), 2,2′,7,7′-Tetrakis (N,N-di-p-methoxyphenylamino)-9,9′-spirobifluorene (spiro-MeOTAD, 99.8%, Borun New Material Technology LTD, China).

PSCs are manufactured by the sequential two-step method following this work [36]. The 1st step used the PbI2 solution (1.3 M) mixed with PbBr2 for 2.5 mol% and dispersed in mixing solution DMF: DMSO with 8:2 vol ratio, then annealed at 70 °C/2 h. Then, perovskite solution was deposited on the ETL surface by spin-coating technique at 2500 rpm/30 s. Next, the 2nd step deposited the FAI: MABr: MACl (240: 30: 30 mg) solution (dispersed in 3 ml IPA at room temperature) for a sequential layer via spin-coating (5000 rpm/30 s) and heated with 150 °C/30 min. Next, the hole transporting layer (HTL) is prepared using Spiro-OMeTAD (72 mg ml−1), dissolved in CB and doped with 4-tert-butyl pyridine (28.8 μl) and LiTFSI solution (17.5 μl) for enhancing the conductivity of HTL. HTL solution was covered on a perovskite layer with spin-coating at 4000 rpm/30 s. Significantly, this process progressed using a homemade glove box with nitrogen gas to control the humidity in the perovskite forming process at room temperature. Lastly, silver (Ag) electrodes were coated on the top of the HTL layer by the thermal evaporation coating method at 9.0 × 10−5 mbar of vacuum level with 1.8 Å s−1 of evaporation rate.

The PSCs have characterized the photovoltaic properties via the I–V test system (Ossila, automated-S211 (T2003B)) with built-in software under simulated solar light AM 1.5 at 100 mW cm−2 on active area PSCs (0.04 cm2) at room temperature. All PSC devices were measured for J–V characteristics by a voltage scan rate of 10 mV/s. Charge dynamics measurements of PSCs were inspected by an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, HIOKI 3522-50 LCR Hi tester).

The GQDs synthesis process obtained the combined molecules of citric acid and GQDs in products, including an adapted length of alkyl chains and various carboxyl groups bonded with GQDs in structures. The size distribution of GQDs by the electrochemical process derived from the TEM image (as shown in an inset) is shown in Fig. 1 (a). A size average is in the range of 1.7 ± 0.2 nm, confirming the small size of quantum dots (supplementary information). XPS spectra of C 1s and O 1s spectra are shown in Fig. 1 (b) and (c), respectively. The C1s spectra in Fig. 1 (b) indicate XPS peaks at 284.40, 286.42, and 288.65 eV, which are assigned to C–C/C=C, C–O, and O–C=O bonds [37], respectively. While O1s spectra in Fig. 1 (c) indicate XPS peaks at 530.32, 531.76, and 533.03 eV and are assigned to C–O, O–H, and H–OH bonds [38] on GQDs. These chemical bonds on GQDs suggest a bonding of carboxyl groups to GQDs. Furthermore, the carboxyl functional groups of GQDs are confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR transmission spectra (Fig. 1 (d)) exhibit the absorption peak at around 3254, 2105, 1639, and 1227 cm−1 and identified to O–H, C≡C, C=C stretch, and C–OH of the carbon bonds in GQDs, respectively. Therefore, GQDs were prepared using an electrochemical process containing the carboxyl functional groups [28], resulting in molecular configurations that bind with the SnO2 surface.

The surface analysis of SnO2 ETLs is performed on the ETLs modified by adding citric acid to study the effect of the carboxyl group and by adding GQDs prepared by the electrochemical process to study the combining effect of both the carboxyl group and GQDs. The analysis is investigated by comparing the unmodified SnO2 ETL. SnO2 is modified with carboxylic acid molecules, namely citric acid, which is incorporated in the carboxyl group structures to examine the effect of molecular structures on the binding surface electronic states of SnO2.

The XPS result was analyzed to examine the chemical interactions of the carboxyl acid and SnO2 molecules. Based on the results in Fig. 2 (a), Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2 peaks located at 486.42 and 494.86 eV [39,40] are monitored as the XPS spectra of the unmodified SnO2 film. For the addition of citric acid and GQDs molecules in SnO2 film, the Sn 3 d peaks of SnO2 films are changed to the stronger binding energies level than the unmodified film, indicating the stabilized bonding of molecule and Sn atom are produced on the SnO2 surface [41]. Interestingly, the Sn 3 d peaks display the shifted energy in the SnO2-citric larger than SnO2-GQDs, implying the stronger binding affinity between carboxylic acid and citric molecules groups in SnO2-citric are produced when compared to the SnO2-GQDs. Meanwhile, this investigation demonstrated the adequate alkyl chain spacing in the citric molecules bonded with the SnO2 surface, resulting in higher binding energy in this case. Significantly, SnO2-GQDs exhibited a poor binding ability from their huge molecular structures due to the GQD molecules being combined with citric acid, affecting the more extended alkyl backbone and causing less anchoring on SnO2. In addition, the higher shifted binding energy of adding citric acid and GQDs in SnO2 films induced a higher negative charge concentration surrounding Sn atoms [42], implying the improvement of surface charge.

Additionally, the O 1s spectra of the unmodified SnO2 and modified SnO2 films in Fig. 2 (b) revealed the two prominent peaks for oxygen species, presenting the binding energy at 530.36 and 531.62 eV [40,43,44] matches to the saturated oxygen (Sn–O–Sn) and the oxygen bonded to an undercoordinated Sn atom (OH− groups on the SnO2 surface), respectively. However, the OH− groups on the SnO2 surface exhibited a defect state (Odef) on the SnO2 surface, which reduces the electron extraction interface via the nonradiative recombination loss [45,46].

Moreover, the O 1s integrating peak area can analyze the ratios of the Odef from the O–H bond signal to the Sn–O–Sn saturated oxide signal. As a result, it can be considered that the reduced ratio from 0.60 to 0.59 of the SnO2-citric case implies surface defects on the citric acid-modified SnO2 films are passivated [28]. Meanwhile, the SnO2-GQDs case exhibited the ratio of O 1s integrating peak area for 0.79, indicating the presence of surface defects by the anchored GQDs molecules on the SnO2 surface assigned as carboxyl molecules effect on the SnO2 surface. The enlarged carboxylic acid groups with proper alkyl-chain spacing in the citric molecule improved the effectiveness of the interaction between Sn-coordinated atoms via the blocking of oxygen vacancy defects around them and isolating the OH− groups on the SnO2 surface [47]. The absence of trap states is estimated to improve the electronic properties of the SnO2 ETL. Consequently, this modification gains defect passivation at ETL.

In addition, adding GQDs in SnO2 can dramatically increase oxygen vacancies [40] with the carboxyl groups from GQDs anchored on the SnO2 surface, leading to a hydrophobic feature on this surface later. This investigation facilitated the reduction of heterogeneous perovskite grains grown on SnO2 film, resulting in improved perovskite grains on ETL films [48].

From UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 2 (c), the modified SnO2 ETLs exhibit the absorption edges at 330, 332, and 335 nm for SnO2, SnO2-citric, and SnO2-GQDs, respectively, with the slightly improved transmittance in the visible range. The higher transmittance of the modified SnO2 ETLs confirms a diminished absorption loss of the PSCs, resulting in higher photocurrent.

Fig. 2 (d) shows the XRD spectra of SnO2 and modified SnO2 films, including SnO2-citric and SnO2-GQDs. The tetragonal phase is observed in all samples with the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 26.4°, 33.5°, 37.7°, 51.4°, 54.5°, 61.5°, and 65.5° correspond to SnO2 (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (112), and (301) planes (COD: 152–6638), which are characteristic of the crystalline structure of SnO2 films [49]. Therefore, the modified SnO2 with citric acid and GQDs for ETLs does not change the phase or crystallinity of SnO2 ETLs.

The wettability of various SnO2 ETL surfaces is examined using contact angle measurements before coating the perovskite precursor solution. Fig. 3 (a)-(c) shows contact angles of unmodified SnO2 ETL (40 ± 1.1°), compared to those of the SnO2-citric and SnO2-GQDs ETLs with higher contact angles of 44 ± 1.5° and 56 ± 2.2°, respectively. The higher contact angle in SnO2-citric ETL compared to unmodified SnO2 film (Fig. 3 (d)), induced by the passivation of surface defects with carboxyl functional groups on the SnO2 surface [28], as shown in the diagram of Fig. 3 (e).

Also, the higher contact angle in SnO2-GQDs ETL is due to the combination of GQDs and carboxyl functional groups anchored on the SnO2 surface, as illuminated in Fig. 3 (f). The existence of carboxyl and GQDs functional groups obtained from the electrochemical synthesis process of GQDs in the citric acid electrolyte, as presented in the FTIR results in Fig. 1 (d). Combining carboxyl and GQDs functional groups leads to non-wetting ability on the surface through covalent modification, resulting in the absence of oxygenated functional groups and the alkylation of GQDs [37]. This supports the efficient applications of GQDs on the SnO2 ETL surface.

The reason for an increase of the hydrophobicity by the functionalization of GQD can be explained in terms of the hydrophilic head (polar head) and hydrophobic tail (non-polar tail), as shown in Fig. 3. For the experiment, GQDs are synthesized by using citric acid as a surface modifier which has carboxyl groups and can be attached with GQDs. Typically, the carboxyl groups of citric acid are anchored to GQDs, and GQD will form a hydrophilic head with the carboxyl groups (-C-OH) as a hydrophobic tail (Fig. 3 (f)) [50]. These hydrophobic tails are surrounded to the outside of GQDs resulting in the more hydrophobic property of GQDs by citric acid modification. Therefore, the SnO2 surface with GQDs modified by citric acid exhibits more hydrophobic properties compared to the unmodified SnO2.

The SEM morphological results of SnO2-citric and SnO2-GQDs-based perovskite films, shown in Fig. 4 (b) and (c), respectively, represent the compacted crystal arrangement with effective interfacial contact in both cases. The thickness between SnO2 and perovskite films is about 40 and 850 nm, respectively.

For the top view, SEM images indicate the morphology of perovskite film placed on various SnO2 ETL films. Fig. 4 (a) shows many grains boundary of perovskite film, forming fully covered on the unmodified SnO2 with an average perovskite grain size boundary of 388 nm (Fig. 4 (d)). Simultaneously, the SnO2 films were modified with citric acid and GQDs molecules, indicating that the grain-size boundary distribution in perovskite films became narrower, resulting in the average perovskite grain sizes increased to 460 nm (Fig. 4 (e)) and 702 nm (Fig. 4 (f)) for the modified with citric acid and GQDs, respectively.

Compared to unmodified SnO2, SnO2-citric exhibits a higher contact angle, implying the reduction of the wettability. However, the SnO2-citric ETL still improves crystallinity and enlarged grain sizes of perovskite films along the networked structure by the modified OH− functional groups on the SnO2 surface, providing lower surface energy. Meanwhile, the modified SnO2 ETL with GQDs reveals the development of a higher water contact angle compared to other conditions. This suggests that carboxyl group molecules functionalize surface wettability from GQDs on the SnO2 ETL surface and facilitate lower surface energy, supporting the expanded grain size of perovskite films like the previous case of SnO2-citric ETL. In addition, the perovskite film covered on the SnO2-GQDs ETL showed better crystallinity and full surface coverage (Fig. 4 (c)). It was impacted by the surface polarity of molecules on the ETL surface, leading to increased thickness and facilitated grain size, as observed in the SEM measurements. Thus, both citric acid and GQDs molecules modified SnO2 ETL contribute to the uniformity of film morphology and improve crystallinity of the perovskite films.

A PSC structure of FTO/ETL/perovskite/Spiro-OMeTAD/Ag, as presented in Fig. 5 (a), is fabricated and investigated. The J-V measurement in reverse scan on PSCs with the different ETL conditions of unmodified SnO2, SnO2-citric, and SnO2-GQDs is presented in Fig. 5 (b), with the essential photovoltaic factors brief in Table 1 .

A steady state photoluminescence (PL) is conducted to investigate the charge transfer property at the electron extraction interface. PL spectra of ETL/perovskite layers are shown in Fig. 6
(a) with the continuous excitation wavelength of 470 nm (λ
exc ). PL peaks at about 784 nm are observed for all SnO2 ETL conditions with different intensities. The highest intensity is obtained for the unmodified SnO2 ETL. The PL quenching (lower PL intensity) is observed by 82% for the SnO2-citric ETL and 84% for the SnO2-GQDs ETL. The PL quenching phenomenon suggests the more effective electron carriers transfer to various modified SnO2 ETLs (citric acid, GQDs) from the perovskite films.

The perovskite film defects are investigated regarding trap density via the space charge limited current (SCLC) measurement. As the trap density (N
trap ) is evaluated by equation (1) [51].
(1)
N
t r a p
=
2 ε
ε 0
V
T F L
e
L 2
where V
TFL is a trap-filled limit voltage defined as the voltage points between an ohmic contact and trap-filled region, ɛ is the dielectric constant of perovskite (ɛ = 46.9) [36], ɛ
0 is the vacuum permittivity, e is the charge of an electron, and L is the thickness of perovskite layer. The trap density is obtained via the I–V measurement of electron-only devices with the structure of FTO/ETL)/Perovskite/PCBM/Ag, as shown in an inset of Fig. 6 (b) under dark conditions. The determined V
TFL values of the unmodified SnO2 ETL are 0.198 V compared to 0.158 V and 0.118 V for those of the SnO2-citric and SnO2-GQDs on perovskite films, resulting in the decrease in trap density from 2.37 × 1015 to 1.89 × 1015 and 1.41 × 1015 cm−3, respectively. The improved trap density on perovskite films is ascribed to the enhanced quality and crystallinity of films [33]. Thus, the lowest trap density is observed in SnO2-GQDs ETL and agrees with the highest PCE.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) provided supplementary insights for the electron charge transfer mechanism at interfaces, demonstrating the phenomenon of charge transporting and recombination resistance at the interface of PSC devices [52]. Fig. 6 (c) shows Nyquist plots of PSC on various SnO2 ETLs with an analysis by the equivalent circuit of the PSC. The detailed fitting parameters are summarized in the inset, where R
1 and R
2 are defined as electron transfer and charge electron recombination resistance, respectively. As a result, the modified SnO2 ETL with GQDs exhibited a lower R
1 and larger R
2 of 13.5 Ω and 729.8 Ω, respectively, implying the improvement by the modified SnO2 ETLs with GQDs compared with other SnO2 ETLs.

Stability tests of the unencapsulated cells using SnO2, SnO2-citric, and SnO2-GQDs ETLs were performed in terms of a normalized PCE as a function of aging time under the same aging conditions, including humidity, temperature, and lighting conditions by storing in the atmosphere at room temperature (25 °C, 30 % humidity). The normalized PCE decreases as a function of aging time, as shown in Fig. 7. T80 (a time at the normalized PCE remaining 80% [53]) is about 14, 20, and 28 days for the unmodified SnO2, SnO2-citric, and SnO2-GQDs ETLs, respectively. Thus, the modified ETLs with citric acid and GQDs exhibit better stability than that of the unmodified SnO2 ETL.

The surface modification of SnO2 ETL is successfully performed by adding citric acid to explore the significance of the carboxyl group and by adding GQDs prepared by the electrochemical method to study the combined effect of both the carboxyl group and GQDs. As a result, the hydrophobic surface of SnO2-GQDs is obtained, and the grain size of the perovskite layer is expanded by using this modification process. Also, the charge trap density and recombination carriers between ETL and perovskite interface are improved. Thus, the surface modification of SnO2-GQDs ETL can enhance the PCE of PSCs with the highest PCE of 17.81% from 15.52% and a decrease of the hysteresis effect. The enhancement is mainly due to the combining effect of carboxyl groups and GQDs interface engineering between ETL and perovskites layers. This suggests that the combining effect can be effectively used in surface modification and for efficiency enhancement of PSCs.

Rangsan Panyathip: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sukrit Sucharitakul: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kritsada Hongsith: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Wakul Bumrungsan: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Vasan Yarangsi: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Surachet Phaduangdhitidhada: Investigation, Data curation. Narong Chanlek: Methodology, Formal analysis. Supab Choopun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research project was supported by Fundamental Fund 2023, Chiang Mai University.
